gmi_articles.title: Paeoniae Radix-containing herbal medicines might promote improvements in restless legs syndrome.
Application of short-term massage with olive oil as a complementary method seems to be effective in reducing the severity of uremic restless legs syndrome.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Primary RLS is chronic; spontaneous remission in mild cases ~30-40% over 5 years; pregnancy-induced RLS remits post-delivery; augmentation on dopaminergic drugs worsens symptoms in 60-80% at 10 years
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Iron IV supplementation in iron-deficient RLS: 57% responders vs 35% placebo (ferric carboxymaltose — Phase 3 positive); alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin enacarbil, pregabalin): 53-55% RLS responders; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole): 70-80% initial response but augmentation in 60-80% at 10 years
remission.gap_size: Moderate — iron deficiency treatment is underused as primary intervention; dopamine agonists chronically overused despite augmentation risk
remission.barrier_detail: IV iron therapy for iron-deficient RLS (<50 µg/L ferritin) is highly effective and underused; dopamine agonists are first-line in most practices despite augmentation being essentially inevitable in long-term use; augmentation causes 24-hour symptoms worse than original disease
remission.last_soc_change: 2023 — updated AAN/AASM guidelines recommending alpha-2-delta ligands over dopamine agonists as first-line (augmentation concern); ferric carboxymaltose approved for iron-deficient RLS; dopamine agonists (ropinirole, pramipexole) as first-line since 2006
remission.outdated_treatments: Dopamine agonists as first-line for long-term RLS management (augmentation in 60-80%; guidelines now recommend alpha-2-delta ligands first-line for chronic RLS); benzodiazepines for RLS (clonazepam — no RCT evidence, tolerance/dependence risk); opioids as first-line (effective but should be reserved for refractory/augmented cases)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol — evidence-based for iron-deficient RLS; dramatically underutilised); magnesium supplementation (modest RCT evidence, safe, commonly used); pneumatic compression devices (RLS relief mechanism: peripheral stimulation suppresses dorsal spinal cord excitability); dietary: ferritin-rich foods, avoid caffeine/alcohol
remission.frontier_signal: Extended-release gabapentin enacarbil (Horizant — established but underutilised); opioid antagonist-augmented therapy (buprenorphine combinations); high-frequency spinal cord stimulation; transcranial magnetic stimulation; CRBN (cereblon) targeted protein degradation for dopaminergic normalisation
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
